revisions.content:
Also referred to as an autopilot or autodrive car, the driverless car is currently only available through science-fiction, but research in the real world is slowly progressing towards the unveiling of a car that will initially be able to navigate on large highways like the U.S. Interstate system, and with later advances progress to being able to navigate through surface roads, and(in theory) off-road terrain as well.

Driverless technology will improve the lives of everyone using cars today by removing the need to concentrate for up to hours at a time on the road and traffic, reducing auto accidents from one of the leading killers of the industrial world to a rare mechanical failure, decreasing travel times by removing the need for human traffic controls and speed limits, and by allowing passengers to spend their time working or relaxing, increasing both leisure time and productivity.

It will also affect the lives of people who don't use the cars. The technology will help economies with more efficient and reliable transportation. For example, there may be "robot delivery" trucks that are dispatched or run circuits round the clock by independent companies to pick up and deliver goods - a local resturant, for example, could take orders over phone or Internet, make the orders, and then pack and ship them on a truck that makes runs within the neighborhood on an hourly or half-hourly basis.

People who turn their cars to destructive purposes(criminals, the angry, suicidal...) will be less able to engage in the kind of behavior with a driverless car. Even assuming a manual mode exists, safety measures will be around to hit the brakes in situations that look lethal, and people who need the protection will be able to order cars that practice defensive driving and can aid them in a combat situation.

The '''driverless car''' is a theoretical technology that would be able to navigate on large highways like the U.S. [[interstate highway]] system, and later on smaller roads or even off-road, with little or no human intervention needed. Also referred to as an autopilot, autonomous vehicle, autodrive car, or an automated guided vehicle (AGV).

Driverless passenger program include the FROG passenger vehicles from Holland, and The Argo research project from Italy. Entries to the US DARPA challenge are off-road autonomous   vehicles.

== Benefits ==

Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.

The technology could help economies with more efficient and reliable transportation. For example, there may be "robot delivery" trucks dispatched round the clock to pick up and deliver goods.

 PRETTY MUCH

The '''driverless car''' is a theoretical technology that would be able to navigate on large highways like the U.S. [[interstate highway]] system, and later on smaller roads or even off-road, with little or no human intervention needed. Also referred to as an autopilot, autonomous vehicle, autodrive car, or an automated guided vehicle (AGV).

Driverless passenger programs include the FROG passenger vehicles from Holland, and The Argo research project from Italy. Entries to the US [[DARPA Grand Challenge]] are off-road autonomous vehicles.

== Benefits ==

* Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.

* The technology could help economies with more efficient and reliable transportation. For example, there may be autonomous or remote-controlled delivery trucks dispatched around the clock to pick up and deliver goods.

* This would also prove useful for car buyers that request a vehicle not sold in their area, or who prefer not to visit a dealership. After entering all the needed information online, the website would say, "Your car is on its way, Mr. Shultz. Thank you for your purchase." Then the car drives itself into your driveway a few minutes to a few days later.

* The driverless car may be able to valet itself or allow a visually impared motorist to travel independently. This would also be convenient for those who leave the country for extended periods and wouldn't want to pay exorbitant parking fees to leave it at the airport; the car would simply drive itself back home. 

* If the car has a long distance to cover and needs to refuel, it would pull itself into a gas station, honk the horn, roll down its own windows, and tell an employee through the car radio that the autonomous car needs refueling, and what grade of fuel it needs. Before or after the attendant refuels it, the car would print out the owner's credit card or checking account number in order to pay for the fuel.

* This would be tremendous Godsend for people that drive to bars and inebriate themselves; the ability for a car to shuttle them home would plummet DUIs and drunken-related accidents through the floor.

The '''driverless car''' is a theoretical technology that would be able to navigate on large highways like the U.S. [[interstate highway]] system, and later on smaller roads or even off-road, with little or no human intervention needed. Also referred to as an autopilot, autonomous vehicle, autodrive car, or an automated guided vehicle (AGV).

Driverless passenger programs include the FROG passenger vehicles from Holland, and the ARGO research project from Italy. Entries to the US [[DARPA Grand Challenge]] are off-road autonomous vehicles.

== Benefits ==

* Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.

* The technology could help economies with more efficient and reliable transportation. For example, there may be autonomous or remote-controlled delivery trucks dispatched around the clock to pick up and deliver goods.

* This would also prove useful for car buyers that request a vehicle not sold in their area, or who prefer not to visit a dealership. After entering all the needed information online, the website would say, "Your car is on its way, Mr. Shultz. Thank you for your purchase." Then the car drives itself into your driveway a few minutes to a few days later.

* The driverless car may be able to valet itself or allow a visually impared motorist to travel independently. This would also be convenient for those who leave the country for extended periods and wouldn't want to pay exorbitant parking fees to leave it at the airport; the car would simply drive itself back home. 

* Conversely, "personal cars" might disappear entirely, instead the entire transportation system acting as an ever-ready taxicab service paid for by taxes and/or advertisements thereon.

* If the car has a long distance to cover and needs to refuel, it would pull itself into a gas station, honk the horn, roll down its own windows, and tell an employee through the car radio that the autonomous car needs refueling, and what grade of fuel it needs. Before or after the attendant refuels it, the car would print out the owner's credit card or checking account number in order to pay for the fuel.

* This would be tremendous Godsend for people that drive to bars and inebriate themselves; the ability for a car to shuttle them home would plummet DUIs and drunken-related accidents through the floor.

* On a related note, as pratically all automobile accidents (particularly fatal ones) are caused by human driver error, driverless cars would effectively eliminate nearly all hazards associated with driving as well as driver fatalities and injuries (driving is currently one of the most deadly forms of transportation with over a million deaths annually worldwide [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_of_the_car]). Additional "hidden" benefits are greatly increased efficiency and personal comfort as cars can be made lighter and more space efficient with the absence of safety technologies rendered obsolete and unnecessarily redundant with computerized driving.

== Disadvantages ==

* At least initially, driverless cars will be more expensive, a problem common to all forms of new and revolutionary technology.

* Also, at least initially, integration issues with current infastructure (or integrating future infastructure for driverless cars) presents a major obstacle. Conflicts between driverless cars and any driver-operated cars still present on the roads will need to be addressed.

* Driving as a personal hobby and sport, and indeed the entire car-oriented "[[sub-culture]]" will effectively be entirely eliminated. Catering industries may very well be eliminated as well, leading to an economic impact. Other industries peripheral to the car industry can be impacted as well (such as the auto insurance industry).

==References==

[http://www.cybercars.org CyberCars]

== Introduction ==

The '''driverless car''' is a theoretical technology that would be able to navigate on large highways like the U.S. [[interstate highway]] system, and later on smaller roads or even off-road, with little or no human intervention needed. Also referred to as an autopilot, autonomous vehicle, autodrive car, or an automated guided vehicle (AGV).

Driverless passenger programs include the FROG passenger vehicles from Holland, and the ARGO research project from Italy. Entries to the US [[DARPA Grand Challenge]] are off-road autonomous vehicles.

'''This article is undergoing a major revision. New information is being added daily.'''

== Motivations ==

=== Benefits ===

* Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.

* The technology could help economies with more efficient and reliable transportation. For example, there may be autonomous or remote-controlled delivery trucks dispatched around the clock to pick up and deliver goods.

* This would also prove useful for car buyers that request a vehicle not sold in their area, or who prefer not to visit a dealership. After entering all the needed information online, the website would say, "Your car is on its way, Mr. Shultz. Thank you for your purchase." Then the car drives itself into your driveway a few minutes to a few days later.

* The driverless car may be able to valet itself or allow a visually impared motorist to travel independently. This would also be convenient for those who leave the country for extended periods and wouldn't want to pay exorbitant parking fees to leave it at the airport; the car would simply drive itself back home. 

* Conversely, "personal cars" might disappear entirely, instead the entire transportation system acting as an ever-ready taxicab service paid for by taxes and/or advertisements thereon.

* If the car has a long distance to cover and needs to refuel, it would pull itself into a gas station, honk the horn, roll down its own windows, and tell an employee through the car radio that the autonomous car needs refueling, and what grade of fuel it needs. Before or after the attendant refuels it, the car would print out the owner's credit card or checking account number in order to pay for the fuel.

* This would be tremendous Godsend for people that drive to bars and inebriate themselves; the ability for a car to shuttle them home would plummet DUIs and drunken-related accidents through the floor.

* On a related note, as pratically all automobile accidents (particularly fatal ones) are caused by human driver error, driverless cars would effectively eliminate nearly all hazards associated with driving as well as driver fatalities and injuries (driving is currently one of the most deadly forms of transportation with over a million deaths annually worldwide [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_of_the_car]). Additional "hidden" benefits are greatly increased efficiency and personal comfort as cars can be made lighter and more space efficient with the absence of safety technologies rendered obsolete and unnecessarily redundant with computerized driving.

* Parents can pre-program the vehicle to pick up their children at the end of the school day, drop them off at soccer practice, and drive itself back to work, ready to take them home at the end of their workday.

*If, for example, a child wants to go to [[Chuck E. Cheese's]] 25 miles away, and a parent is too busy to take them, they can just ask the parent to program the car to shuttle them there, return, and re-send itself there and pick them up at the preprogrammed time. All the programing can even be done on the PC, via Internet, and not necessarily in the car.

=== Disadvantages ===

* At least initially, driverless cars will be more expensive, a problem common to all forms of new and revolutionary technology.

* Also, at least initially, integration issues with current infastructure (or integrating future infastructure for driverless cars) presents a major obstacle. Conflicts between driverless cars and any driver-operated cars still present on the roads will need to be addressed.

* Driving as a personal hobby and sport, and indeed the entire car-oriented "[[sub-culture]]" will effectively be entirely eliminated. Catering industries may very well be eliminated as well, leading to an economic impact. Other industries peripheral to the car industry can be impacted as well (such as the auto insurance industry).

== Approaches ==

=== Open-space, Military ===

This includes the US Military [[DARPA Grand Challenge]] and a couple of [http://www.isracast.com/tech_news/080405_tech.htm Israeli projects].

=== Lane-Following ===

These technologies will take over your car and drive for you on any stretch of the highway that is compatible with their technology.

These project are of two types: 
*Cars following the normal (painted) lane marks in an unmodified highway, e.g. ARGO (Italian); 
*Construction of special lanes with specific infrastructure, e.g. magnets in the [http://www.govtech.net/magazine/story.php?id=95445&issue=10:1997 AHS project], in San Diego, California 1997, or a special monorail in the [http://www.ruf.dk/ Danish Project].

=== Driver-assist ===
Driver-assist mechanisms are of two distinct types, Those who take over control of a car at some point in time, like The (Vehicle-only based) Honda and Vauxhall "followers"; OTOH there are systems that ONLY assist in driving, like
*Vehicle-only warning systems
*Infrastructure-based, Japanese gov. (Links, Links!)
These technologies are not strictly "Driverless Cars" since the car never actually drives itself. However, they are worth discussing as they are seen as stepping stones towards truly driver;ess cars.

== Technologies ==

=== Sensors ===

Mobileye, Japanese Infra-red article, Some stuff off the DARPA challenge....

=== Navigation ===

Mainly solved, GPS-related systems

=== Micro-Navigation ===

Here is the real problem. We can go here onto a big discussion of the "Navigation Problem" (Which should have its own WikiPedia Page) look at http://nips.djvuzone.org/

=== Control of Vehicle ===

[[Category:Automobiles]]

[[de:Fahrerloses Transportfahrzeug]]

The '''driverless car''' is an emerging family of technologies, ultimately aimed at a full "taxi-like" 
experience for car users. This, together with [[Alternative Propulsion]] is seen as the main technological
advance expected leading into 2020. These projects are also referred to as an autopilot, autonomous vehicle, autodrive car, or an automated guided vehicle (AGV).

Driverless passenger programs include the FROG passenger vehicles from Holland, the ARGO research project from Italy, and the [[DARPA Grand Challenge]] from the USA.

{{Underconstruction}}

== Motivations ==

=== Benefits ===

* Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.
* These benifits will improve Private and commercial life, improving the overall economy (like any technology)
* Self-deliver of cars to/from dealerships, garages, train stations, airports, schools...
* The driverless car may allow a visually impared motorist to travel independently. 
* This would be tremendous Godsend for people that drive to bars and inebriate themselves; the ability for a car to shuttle them home would plummet DUIs and drunken-related accidents through the floor.

=== Disadvantages ===

* At least initially, driverless cars will be more expensive, a problem common to most revolutionary technologies.
* Driving as a personal hobby and sport, and indeed the entire car-oriented "[[sub-culture]]" will effectively be entirely eliminated.
* Driving and Catering industries may very well be eliminated as well, leading to an economic impact. Other industries peripheral to the car industry can be impacted as well (such as the auto insurance industry).

== The Problem ==

Though the vision of a fully-autonomous vehicle is clear it would be
such an upheaval in technolgy and lifestyle that few dare
contemplate a "Big Bang" new technology that would simply "do it". From a scientific/engineering Point of view,
this looks
like a case of an [[AI-complete]] problem, meanning that it is so complex it "can never be done".

However, a few brave souls are attempting to solve bits and pieces
of the problem - either for the benefit of the limited invention created, 
or explicitly as stepping stones towards a fully Driverless Car. Though most of the projects are government-sponsored, there is already a significant involvement from the private sector.

The challenges involved in realising this vision can broadly be divided into the technical and the social.
The technical problems are broadly in the design of the sensors and control system required to
make such a car work. The social challenge is in getting [[Joe Bloggs]] to trust the car, getting legislators to permit the car onto the public roads, and untangling the legal issues of
liability for any mishaps with no person in charge.

== Technologies Required for a Full Solution ==

=== Sensors ===

Mobileye, Japanese Infra-red article, Some stuff off the DARPA challenge....

=== Navigation ===

Mainly solved, GPS-related systems

=== Micro-Navigation ===

Here is the real problem. We can go here onto a big discussion of the "Navigation Problem" (Which should have its own WikiPedia Page) look at http://nips.djvuzone.org/

=== Control of Vehicle ===

== Projects so far ==

=== Open-space, Military ===

This includes the:
*US Military [[DARPA Grand Challenge]]
*[[European Land-Robot Trial (ELROB)]]
*a couple of [http://www.isracast.com/tech_news/080405_tech.htm Israeli projects].

=== Lane-Following ===

These technologies will take over your car and drive for you on any stretch of the highway that is compatible with their technology.

These project are of two types: 
*Cars following the normal (painted) lane marks in an unmodified highway, e.g. ARGO (Italian); 
*Construction of special lanes with specific infrastructure, e.g. magnets in the [http://www.govtech.net/magazine/story.php?id=95445&issue=10:1997 AHS project], in San Diego, California 1997, or a special monorail in the [http://www.ruf.dk/ Danish Project].

=== Driver-assist ===
Driver-assist mechanisms are of several distinct types, those that:
*Follow another car on a motorway ("Enhanced" or "adaptive" cruise control), like The [http://www.ford.com/en/innovation/safety/accidentAvoidance/adaptiveCruiseControl.htm Ford], Honda or [http://www.autoexpress.co.uk/news/62349/driverless_vectra_is_go.html Vauxhall](GM).
*only advise, inform or warn the driver, like
**Vehicle-only warning systems (EXAMLES!, like volvo's "you are 25cm from the car behind")
**Infrastructure-based, driver warning/information-giving systems, such as those developed by the Japanese government (Links, Links!)
*modify driving instruction given by the Human driver for Safety, like ABS and its more recent developments, see  [[Car_safety#Safety_features|Safety Features]].
*Automatically park the car, e.g. [http://www.i4u.com/article590.html from Toyota].

These technologies are not strictly "Driverless Cars" since the car never actually drives itself. However, they are worth a mention as they are seen as stepping stones towards truly driverless cars.

== Social Solutions ==

Not much here.

== Discussion & Future ==

[[Category:Automobiles]]

[[de:Fahrerloses Transportfahrzeug]]

The '''driverless car''' is an emerging family of technologies, ultimately aimed at a full "taxi-like" 
experience for car users. This, together with [[Alternative propulsion]] is seen as the main technological
advance expected leading into 2020. These projects are also referred to as an autopilot, autonomous vehicle, autodrive car, or an automated guided vehicle (AGV).

Driverless passenger programs include the FROG passenger vehicles from Holland, the ARGO research project from Italy, and the [[DARPA Grand Challenge]] from the USA. See also [[Smart car]]s.

{{Underconstruction}}


== The Challenge ==

Though the vision of a fully-autonomous vehicle is clear it would be
such an upheaval in technolgy and lifestyle that few dare
contemplate a "Big Bang" new technology that would simply "do it". From a scientific/engineering Point of view,
this looks
like a case of an [[AI-complete]] problem, meanning that it is so complex it "can never be done".

However, a few brave souls are attempting to solve bits and pieces
of the problem - either for the benefit of the limited invention created, 
or explicitly as stepping stones towards a fully Driverless Car. Though most of the projects are government-sponsored, there is already a significant involvement from the private sector.

The challenges involved in realising this vision can broadly be divided into the technical and the social.
The technical problems are broadly in the design of the sensors and control system required to
make such a car work. The social challenge is in getting [[Joe Bloggs]] to trust the car, getting legislators to permit the car onto the public roads, and untangling the legal issues of
liability for any mishaps with no person in charge.

Since the social issues have not been addressed in any concerted way yet, we will now turn to a discussion of the technologies, leaving the social issues to the end of this article.

== The elements of any Solution ==
The dream of a Driverless car seems fantastic, and therefore remote. However, 
any solution can be broken down to several sub-systems:

'''Sensors,''' so the car can know where it is and what is around it

'''Navigation''' - How to get to the target location from the present location

'''Micro-Navigation''' - getting through the next few meters, steering, avoiding obstacles, 
abiding by [[rules of the road]], avoiding harm to the vehicle and others.

'''Control''' of the vehicle itself - Actuating the descisions of the Micro-Navigation.

In examining every proposed solution, one should look at the following questions:
*Is this truely a complete system? Does it drive itself "Door-to-Door"?
*To what degree is the proposed solution a step towards the complete vision, or is it just an ad-hoc trick?
*Is the car "autonomous", or would it need changes to the infrastructure?
*How feasible (technically, economically, politically) would it be to deploy the entire solution?
*Can the system allow for and include existing vehicles driven by Humans, or does it need a "clean space"?
*How would it cope with unexpected circustances?

Now, whithout further ado, lets look at what has already been achieved.

== Projects so far ==

=== Open-space, Military ===

This includes the:
*US Military [[DARPA Grand Challenge]]
*[[European Land-Robot Trial (ELROB)]]
*A couple of [http://www.isracast.com/tech_news/080405_tech.htm Israeli projects].
** [http://www.elbitsystems.com/newsRoomPressRin.asp?s=1&id=83&pr=1&s_year=2004&s_month=all Elbit]
** [http://www.iai.co.il/Default.aspx?docID=31663&FolderID=17483&lang=en&res=0&pos=0 IAI's "Guardium"].

=== Lane-Following ===

These technologies will take over your car and drive for you on any stretch of the highway that is compatible with their technology.

These project are of two types: 
*Cars following the normal (painted) lane marks in an unmodified highway, e.g. ARGO (Italian); 
*Construction of special lanes with specific infrastructure, e.g. magnets in the [http://www.govtech.net/magazine/story.php?id=95445&issue=10:1997 AHS project], in San Diego, California 1997, or a special monorail in the [http://www.ruf.dk/ Danish Project].

=== Driver-assist ===
Driver-assist mechanisms are of several distinct types, those that:
*Follow another car on a motorway ("Enhanced" or "adaptive" cruise control), like The [http://www.ford.com/en/innovation/safety/accidentAvoidance/adaptiveCruiseControl.htm Ford], Honda or [http://www.autoexpress.co.uk/news/62349/driverless_vectra_is_go.html Vauxhall](GM). 
*only advise, inform or warn the driver, like
**Vehicle-only warning systems (EXAMLES!, like volvo's "you are 25cm from the car behind")
**Infrastructure-based, driver warning/information-giving systems, such as those developed by the Japanese government (Links, Links!)
*modify driving instruction given by the Human driver for Safety, like ABS and its more recent developments, see  [[Car_safety#Safety_features|Safety Features]].
*Automatically park the car, e.g. [http://www.i4u.com/article590.html from Toyota].

These technologies are not strictly "Driverless Cars" since the car never actually drives itself. However, they are worth a mention as they are seen as stepping stones towards truly driverless cars.

A good collection of these technologies is available at Automotive component manufacturer's sites, such as [http://www.siemensvdo.com/topics/adas/ SiemensVDO] or [http://delphi.com/manufacturers/auto/safesecure/warning/ Delphi (Ford)]. An overview is available [http://www.popsci.com/popsci/futurecar/f2909aa138b84010vgnvcm1000004eecbccdrcrd.html Here].

Interesting stuff from [http://www.worldcarfans.com/news.cfm/newsID/2050819.004/country/gcf/opel/opel-presents-innovations-for-tomorrow's-mobility GM-Opel].

== Summary of existing and missing technologies ==
=== Sensors ===

Mobileye, Japanese Infra-red article, Some stuff off the DARPA challenge....

=== Navigation ===

Mainly solved, GPS-related systems

Here is the real problem. We can go here onto a big discussion of the "Navigation Problem" (Which should have its own WikiPedia Page) look at http://nips.djvuzone.org/ . Look also at [[Navigation research]]

== Social Aspects ==

* getting [[Joe Bloggs]] to trust the car
* getting legislators to permit the car onto the public roads
* untangling the legal issues of liability for any mishaps with no person in charge.

=== Motivations ===

==== Benefits ====

* Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.
* These benefits will improve Private and commercial life, improving the overall economy (like any technology)
* This would be tremendous Godsend for people that drive to bars and inebriate themselves; the ability for a car to shuttle them home would plummet DUIs and drunken-related accidents through the floor.
* The technology could help economies with more efficient and reliable transportation. For example, there may be autonomous or remote-controlled delivery trucks dispatched around the clock to pick up and deliver goods.
* This would also prove useful for car buyers that request a vehicle not sold in their area, or who prefer not to visit a dealership. After entering all the needed information online, the website would say, "Your car is on its way, Mr. Samfreed. Thank you for your purchase." Then the car drives itself into your driveway a few minutes to a few days later.
* The driverless car may be able to valet itself or allow a visually impared motorist to travel independently. This would also be convenient for those who leave the country for extended periods and wouldn't want to pay exorbitant parking fees to leave it at the airport; the car would simply drive itself back home. 
* If the car has a long distance to cover and needs to refuel, it would pull itself into a gas station, honk the horn, roll down its own windows, and tell an employee through the car radio that the autonomous car needs refueling, and what grade of fuel it needs. Before or after the attendant refuels it, the car would print out the owner's credit card or checking account number in order to pay for the fuel.
* On a related note, as pratically all automobile accidents (particularly fatal ones) are caused by human driver error, driverless cars would effectively eliminate nearly all hazards associated with driving as well as driver fatalities and injuries (driving is currently one of the most deadly forms of transportation with over a million deaths annually worldwide [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_of_the_car]). Additional "hidden" benefits are greatly increased efficiency and personal comfort as cars can be made lighter and more space efficient with the absence of safety technologies rendered obsolete and unnecessarily redundant with computerized driving.
* Parents can pre-program the vehicle to pick up their children at the end of the school day, drop them off at soccer practice, and drive itself back to work, ready to take them home at the end of their workday.
*If, for example, a child wants to go to [[Chuck E. Cheese's]] 25 miles away, and a parent is too busy to take them, they can just ask the parent to program the car to shuttle them there, return, and re-send itself there and pick them up at the preprogrammed time. All the programing can even be done on the PC, via Internet, and not necessarily in the car.

==== Costs ====

* At least initially, driverless cars will be more expensive, a problem common to most revolutionary technologies.
* Driving as a personal hobby and sport, and indeed the entire car-oriented "[[sub-culture]]" will effectively be entirely eliminated.
* Driving and Catering industries may very well be eliminated as well, leading to an economic impact. Other industries peripheral to the car industry can be impacted as well (such as the auto insurance industry).

[[Category:Automobiles]]

[[de:Fahrerloses Transportfahrzeug]]

The '''driverless car''' is an emerging family of technologies, ultimately aimed at a full "taxi-like" 
experience for car users. This, together with [[Alternative propulsion]] is seen as the main technological
advance expected leading into 2020. These projects are also referred to as an autopilot, autonomous vehicle, autodrive car, or an automated guided vehicle (AGV).

Driverless passenger programs include the FROG passenger vehicles from Holland, the ARGO research project from Italy, and the [[DARPA Grand Challenge]] from the USA. See also [[Smart car]]s.

{{Underconstruction}}


== The Challenge ==

Though the vision of a fully-autonomous vehicle is clear it would be
such an upheaval in technolgy and lifestyle that few dare
contemplate a "Big Bang" new technology that would simply "do it". From a scientific/engineering Point of view,
this looks
like a case of an [[AI-complete]] problem, meanning that it is so complex it "can never be done".

However, a few brave souls are attempting to solve bits and pieces
of the problem - either for the benefit of the limited invention created, 
or explicitly as stepping stones towards a fully Driverless Car. Though most of the projects are government-sponsored, there is already a significant involvement from the private sector.

The challenges involved in realising this vision can broadly be divided into the technical and the social.
The technical problems are broadly in the design of the sensors and control system required to
make such a car work. The social challenge is in getting [[Joe Bloggs]] to trust the car, getting legislators to permit the car onto the public roads, and untangling the legal issues of
liability for any mishaps with no person in charge.

Since the social issues have not been addressed in any concerted way yet, we will now turn to a discussion of the technologies, leaving the social issues to the end of this article.

== The elements of any Solution ==
The dream of a Driverless car seems fantastic, and therefore remote. However, 
any solution can be broken down to several sub-systems:

'''Sensors,''' so the car can know where it is and what is around it

'''Navigation''' - How to get to the target location from the present location

'''Micro-Navigation''' - getting through the next few meters, steering, avoiding obstacles, 
abiding by [[rules of the road]], avoiding harm to the vehicle and others.

'''Control''' of the vehicle itself - Actuating the decisions of the Micro-Navigation.

In examining every proposed solution, one should look at the following questions:
*Is this truly a complete system? Does it drive itself "Door-to-Door"?
*To what degree is the proposed solution a step towards the complete vision, or is it just an ad-hoc trick?
*Is the car "autonomous", or would it need changes to the infrastructure?
*How feasible (technically, economically, politically) would it be to deploy the entire solution?
*Can the system allow for and include existing vehicles driven by Humans, or does it need a "clean space"?
*How would it cope with unexpected circustances?

Now, whithout further ado, lets look at what has already been achieved.

== Projects so far ==

The work done so far varies significantly in its ambition and its demands in terms of modification
of the infrastructure. Broadly, there are three approaches - The first group to be discussed here is the fully-autonomous vehicles (DARPA, ARGO) - which are the most ambitious, but none are deployed. The second approach uses various enhancements to the infrastructure (either an entire area, or specific lanes) to create a self-driving closed system. Such systems already function in many airports, underground commuter railroads, and some European towns. The Third approach is to incrementally remove requirements from the Human driver, by various "assistance" systems. This approach is slowly trickling into standard cars (e.g. improvements to cruise control).


=== Fully Autonomous ===

These technologies are the most ambitious: The allow a car to drive itself following a pre-set target, until it gets there all on its own. The downside of these seeming marvels is that they are very limted in terms of the environment in which they can operate: Either a desert (free of any Human or Human-mae obstacle), or a clearly-marked, well-painted (in our case Italian) motorway, or Autostrada as they would have it. Therefore, the real benefits of door-to-door are as remote as ever.

=== Pre-Built Infrastructure ===
==== Lanes ====

These projects are of two types: 
*Allocation of special lanes on existing motorways with specific infrastructure, e.g. magnets in the [http://www.govtech.net/magazine/story.php?id=95445&issue=10:1997 AHS project], in San Diego, California 1997
**http://ivsource.net/archivep/2002/feb/020203_AHSeditorial.html

*A special monorail in the [http://www.ruf.dk/ Danish Project]. See also [http://www.galtglobalreview.com/newtech/driverless_cabs_2.html This] from the USA.

*[http://www.buick.co.uk/biway/ BiWay] (UK, private initiative)

Side-note - The Europeans seem to be more at home with rail-based systems (like in general, existing transport) while the Americans (who invented both the Automtic GearBox and Cruise Control) feel happier with cars that look like cars.

==== "Free Ranging ON Grid" ====
The [http://www.frog.nl/ FROG] company from Holland uses a combination of a low-autonomy vehicle with a supervisory central system. The company's purpose-built electric vehicles locate themselves using odometry, recalibrating themselves occasionally using a "maze" of magnets embedded in the environment, and dGPS. The cars avoid collisions using infrared and leser sensors.

The supervision of the vehicles, their navigation and adherence the any rules of the road are done by a centralised computer system. Such a system is well suited to manageing the traffic in a limited space (as is the reliance on magnets) but unsuited for running 10s or 100s of tousands of cars in a full city.

The FROG system is deployed for industrial purposes in factory sites, and as a pilot public transport system in several cities, not least Rotterdam, where the system experienced an [http://www.wolfstad.com/2005/12/driveless-robot-busses-crash/ accident] that proved to be caused by a
[http://www.wolfstad.com/2005/12/driverless-robot-buses-crash-part-2/ Human error](!).

FROG is one of few fully-commercial companies in this field.

=== Driver-assist ===
Driver-assist mechanisms are of several distinct types, those that:
*Follow another car on a motorway ("Enhanced" or "adaptive" cruise control), like The [http://www.ford.com/en/innovation/safety/accidentAvoidance/adaptiveCruiseControl.htm Ford], Honda or [http://www.autoexpress.co.uk/news/62349/driverless_vectra_is_go.html Vauxhall](GM). 
*only advise, inform or warn the driver, like
**Vehicle-only warning systems (EXAMLES!, like volvo's "you are 25cm from the car behind")
**Infrastructure-based, driver warning/information-giving systems, such as those developed by the Japanese government (Links, Links!)
*modify driving instruction given by the Human driver for Safety, like ABS and its more recent developments, see  [[Car safety#Safety features|Safety Features]].
*Automatically park the car, e.g. [http://www.i4u.com/article590.html from Toyota].

These technologies are not strictly "Driverless Cars" since the car never actually drives itself. However, they are worth a mention as they are seen as stepping stones towards truly driverless cars.

A good collection of these technologies is available at Automotive component manufacturer's sites, such as [http://www.siemensvdo.com/topics/adas/ SiemensVDO] or [http://delphi.com/manufacturers/auto/safesecure/warning/ Delphi (Ford)]. An overview is available [http://www.popsci.com/popsci/futurecar/f2909aa138b84010vgnvcm1000004eecbccdrcrd.html Here].

Interesting stuff from [http://www.worldcarfans.com/news.cfm/newsID/2050819.004/country/gcf/opel/opel-presents-innovations-for-tomorrow's-mobility GM-Opel].

A good summary of how far things have progressed '''without''' any true automated driving is provided by [http://economist.com/science/displaystory.cfm?story_id=4316021 The Economist]

=== Motivations ===

==== Benefits ====

* Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.
* These benefits will improve Private and commercial life, improving the overall economy (like any technology)
* This would be tremendous Godsend for people that drive to bars and inebriate themselves; the ability for a car to shuttle them home would plummet DUIs and drunken-related accidents through the floor.

* This would also prove useful for car buyers that request a vehicle not sold in their area, or who prefer not to visit a dealership. After entering all the needed information online, the website would say, "Your car is on its way, Mr. Samfreed. Thank you for your purchase." Then the car drives itself into your driveway a few minutes to a few days later.
* The driverless car may be able to valet itself or allow a visually impared motorist to travel independently. This would also be convenient for those who leave the country for extended periods and wouldn't want to pay exorbitant parking fees to leave it at the airport; the car would simply drive itself back home. 
* If the car has a long distance to cover and needs to refuel, it would pull itself into a gas station, honk the horn, roll down its own windows, and tell an employee through the car radio that the autonomous car needs refueling, and what grade of fuel it needs. Before or after the attendant refuels it, the car would print out the owner's credit card or checking account number in order to pay for the fuel.
* On a related note, as pratically all automobile accidents (particularly fatal ones) are caused by human driver error, driverless cars would effectively eliminate nearly all hazards associated with driving as well as driver fatalities and injuries (driving is currently one of the most deadly forms of transportation with over a million deaths annually worldwide [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_of_the_car]). Additional "hidden" benefits are greatly increased efficiency and personal comfort as cars can be made lighter and more space efficient with the absence of safety technologies rendered obsolete and unnecessarily redundant with computerized driving.
* Parents can pre-program the vehicle to pick up their children at the end of the school day, drop them off at soccer practice, and drive itself back to work, ready to take them home at the end of their workday.
*If, for example, a child wants to go to [[Chuck E. Cheese's]] 25 miles away, and a parent is too busy to take them, they can just ask the parent to program the car to shuttle them there, return, and re-send itself there and pick them up at the preprogrammed time. All the programing can even be done on the PC, via Internet, and not necessarily in the car.

==== Costs ====

* At least initially, driverless cars will be more expensive, a problem common to most revolutionary technologies.
* Driving as a personal hobby and sport, and indeed the entire car-oriented "[[sub-culture]]" will effectively be entirely eliminated.
* Driving and Catering industries may very well be eliminated as well, leading to an economic impact. Other industries peripheral to the car industry can be impacted as well (such as the auto insurance industry).


== Discussion & Future ==

Some systems control everything centrally, and in some the vehicle is truly autonomous in the sense that it "thinks" about its own situation in the first person - such a system can integrate with Humans that think in first person.

Conversely. a system that centrally manages everything, though easier to build from a conceptual and engineering point of view, would face horrendeous economic barriers because of the costs of converting an entire city or country to the new system at once. In order to be compatible with Humans the "first person" POV is key.

This approach is slowly trickling into standard cars (e.g. improvements to cruise control).


=== Fully Autonomous ===

These technologies are the most ambitious: The allow a car to drive itself following a pre-set target, until it gets there all on its own. The downside of these seeming marvels is that they are very limted in terms of the environment in which they can operate: Either a desert (free of any Human or Human-mae obstacle), or a clearly-marked, well-painted (in our case Italian) motorway, or Autostrada as they would have it. Therefore, the real benefits of door-to-door are as remote as ever.

==== Free-ranging military vehicles ====

There are 3 clusters of activity relating to free-ranging off-road cars. All these projects are military-oriented.
*US Military [[DARPA Grand Challenge]]
The US Depertment of Defense announced on the Jul 30, 2002 a "Grand Challenge",
for US-based teams to produce a vehicle that can autonomously navigate and
reach a target in the desert of the southwestern USA.

In March 2004 the first competition was held, for a prize-money of $1m. Not one of the 25 entrants completed the
course. However, in October 2005 five different teams completed the 135-mile (217km) course, and the [[Stanford University]] team won the $2m prize.

Following the 2004 failure, in which several cars were distracted by the "race" to the detriment of basic technology that would allow for actual completion, the 2005 teams were focused on the challenge at hand, and did not seek to develop generic solutions, or a particularly speedy car.

By and large, the sensors used were stabilised in order to avoid the vibration of desert driving. The sensors were based on Visual, Radar, and laser techniologies. The navigational course was pre-programmed, and the micro-navigation and obstacle avoidance were handled by on-board computers - many of the entrants used 8 or more computers to manage the car(!). Though the vehicles were equiped to avoid collision, they did not have any notion of rules-of-the road - but simply regarded each other as moving obstacles.
 
For a more complete description the the Darpa Grand Challenge see the [[DARPA Grand Challenge|main article]], the official [http://www.darpa.mil/grandchallenge/ web site] and [http://news.ninemsn.com.au/article.aspx?id=66463 the] [http://news.zdnet.com/2100-1040_22-5890476.html press] [http://news.zdnet.com/2100-9584_22-5892115.html coverage].

*[[European Land-Robot Trial (ELROB)]]
Not to be outdone by the USA, the German Dept. of Defense announces an event similar to the DARPA grand challenge, 
to be held in May 2006. The event will include both desrt-like scenarios like in the USA, and also urban scenarios in which the vehicle will explore streets and buildings.


*The [[Israel]]i [[Military-Industrial Complex]]
As a followup from its success with [[Unmanned Combat Air Vehicle]]s, and following the construction of
the [[Israeli West Bank barrier]] there has been significant interst in developing a fully-automated border-partol vehicle. Two projects, by 
[http://www.elbitsystems.com/newsRoomPressRin.asp?s=1&id=83&pr=1&s_year=2004&s_month=all Elbit Systems] and 
[http://www.iai.co.il/Default.aspx?docID=31663&FolderID=17483&lang=en&res=0&pos=0 Israel Aircraft Industries]
are both based on the locally-produced [http://www.defense-update.com/products/t/tomcar-armored.htm Armored "Tomcar"]
and have the specific purpose of partolling barier fences against intrusions.

The "SciAutonics II" team in the 2004 DARPA Challenge used Elbit's version of the Tomcar.

==== ARGO ====

ARGO is an Italian project (1996-2001) to allow a cat to follow the normal (painted) lane marks in an unmodified highway, [http://www.argo.ce.unipr.it/ARGO/english/ ARGO]. The culmination of the project was a voyage of 2,000 km during 6 days in the motorways of northern Italy, with an average speed of 90km/h. 94% of the time the car was in fully automatic mode, with the longest automatic strecth being 54 km long.

The ARGO vehicle, a modified [[Lancia_Thema]], had only two Black-and-White video cameras on board, and use [[Stereoscopy|stereoscopic vision]] algorithms to understand its environment. This is in stark contrast to the "laser, radar - whatever you need" approach taken by other efforts in the field.

The project was run by the universities of Parma and Pavia, coordinated by [http://www.ce.unipr.it/~broggi/ Alberto Broggi], and financed by the Italian government.

=== Pre-Built Infrastructure ===

The following projects were conceived as practical attempts to use available technology
in an incremental manner to solve specific problems, like transport within a defined campus area, or driving along
a stretch of motorway. The technologies are proven, and the main barrier to widespread
implememntation is the cost
of deploying the infrastrcuture.

==== MonoRail ====

*A special monorail in the [http://www.ruf.dk/ Danish Project]. See also [http://www.galtglobalreview.com/newtech/driverless_cabs_2.html This] from the USA.

*[http://www.buick.co.uk/biway/ BiWay] (UK, private initiative)

Side-note - The Europeans seem to be more at home with rail-based systems (like in general, existing transport) while the Americans (who invented both the Automtic GearBox and Cruise Control) feel happier with cars that look like cars.

==== Dedicated Lanes ====

Allocation of special lanes on existing motorways with specific infrastructure, e.g. magnets in the [http://www.govtech.net/magazine/story.php?id=95445&issue=10:1997 AHS project], demonstrated
in San Diego, California 1997
**http://ivsource.net/archivep/2002/feb/020203_AHSeditorial.html
**http://faculty.washington.edu/jbs/itrans/bishopahs.htm
**http://www.memagazine.org/backissues/may98/features/smarter/smarter.html
**http://www.pbs.org/wgbh/nova/escape/piocar.html

==== "Free Ranging On Grid" ====
The [http://www.frog.nl/ FROG] company from Holland uses a combination of a low-autonomy vehicle with a supervisory central system. The company's purpose-built electric vehicles locate themselves using odometry, recalibrating themselves occasionally using a "maze" of magnets embedded in the environment, and dGPS. The cars avoid collisions using infrared and leser sensors.

The supervision of the vehicles, their navigation and adherence the any rules of the road are done by a centralised computer system. Such a system is well suited to manageing the traffic in a limited space (as is the reliance on magnets) but unsuited for running 10s or 100s of tousands of cars in a full city.

The FROG system is deployed for industrial purposes in factory sites, and as a pilot public transport system in several cities, not least Rotterdam, where the system experienced an [http://www.wolfstad.com/2005/12/driveless-robot-busses-crash/ accident] that proved to be caused by a
[http://www.wolfstad.com/2005/12/driverless-robot-buses-crash-part-2/ Human error](!).

FROG is one of few fully-commercial companies in this field.

=== Driver-assist ===
Driver-assist mechanisms are of several distinct types, those that:
*Follow another car on a motorway ("Enhanced" or "adaptive" cruise control), like The [http://www.ford.com/en/innovation/safety/accidentAvoidance/adaptiveCruiseControl.htm Ford], Honda or [http://www.autoexpress.co.uk/news/62349/driverless_vectra_is_go.html Vauxhall](GM). 
*only advise, inform or warn the driver, like
**Vehicle-only warning systems (EXAMLES!, like volvo's "you are 25cm from the car behind")
**Infrastructure-based, driver warning/information-giving systems, such as those developed by the Japanese government (Links, Links!)
*modify driving instruction given by the Human driver for Safety, like ABS and its more recent developments, see  [[Car safety#Safety features|Safety Features]].
*Automatically park the car, e.g. [http://www.i4u.com/article590.html from Toyota].

These technologies are not strictly "Driverless Cars" since the car never actually drives itself. However, they are worth a mention as they are seen as stepping stones towards truly driverless cars.

A good collection of these technologies is available at Automotive component manufacturer's sites, such as [http://www.siemensvdo.com/topics/adas/ SiemensVDO] or [http://delphi.com/manufacturers/auto/safesecure/warning/ Delphi (Ford)]. An overview is available [http://www.popsci.com/popsci/futurecar/f2909aa138b84010vgnvcm1000004eecbccdrcrd.html Here].

Interesting stuff from [http://www.worldcarfans.com/news.cfm/newsID/2050819.004/country/gcf/opel/opel-presents-innovations-for-tomorrow's-mobility GM-Opel].

A good summary of how far things have progressed '''without''' any true automated driving is provided by [http://economist.com/science/displaystory.cfm?story_id=4316021 The Economist]

== Summary of existing and missing technologies ==
=== Sensors ===

Mobileye, ARGO's "video only", Japanese Infra-red article, Some stuff off the DARPA challenge....

=== Navigation ===

Mainly solved, GPS-related systems

=== Micro-Navigation ===

Here is the real problem. We can go here onto a big discussion of the "Navigation Problem" (Which should have its own WikiPedia Page) look at http://nips.djvuzone.org/ . Look also at [[Navigation research]]

=== Control of Vehicle ===

== Social Aspects ==

* getting [[Joe Bloggs]] to trust the car
* getting legislators to permit the car onto the public roads
* untangling the legal issues of liability for any mishaps with no person in charge.

=== Despair of progress in the foreseeable future ===
* The UK government seems to see little progress until 2056. See [http://networks.silicon.com/mobile/0,39024665,39155962,00.htm this] and [http://news.cnet.co.uk/networking/0,39029686,49249085,00.htm This].

=== Motivations ===

==== Benefits ====

* Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.
* These benefits will improve Private and commercial life, improving the overall economy (like any technology)
* This would be tremendous Godsend for people that drive to bars and inebriate themselves; the ability for a car to shuttle them home would plummet DUIs and drunken-related accidents through the floor.
* The technology could help economies with more efficient and reliable transportation. For example, there may be autonomous or remote-controlled delivery trucks dispatched around the clock to pick up and deliver goods.
* This would also prove useful for car buyers that request a vehicle not sold in their area, or who prefer not to visit a dealership. After entering all the needed information online, the website would say, "Your car is on its way, Mr. Samfreed. Thank you for your purchase." Then the car drives itself into your driveway a few minutes to a few days later.
* The driverless car may be able to valet itself or allow a visually impared motorist to travel independently. This would also be convenient for those who leave the country for extended periods and wouldn't want to pay exorbitant parking fees to leave it at the airport; the car would simply drive itself back home. 
* If the car has a long distance to cover and needs to refuel, it would pull itself into a gas station, honk the horn, roll down its own windows, and tell an employee through the car radio that the autonomous car needs refueling, and what grade of fuel it needs. Before or after the attendant refuels it, the car would print out the owner's credit card or checking account number in order to pay for the fuel.
* On a related note, as pratically all automobile accidents (particularly fatal ones) are caused by human driver error, driverless cars would effectively eliminate nearly all hazards associated with driving as well as driver fatalities and injuries (driving is currently one of the most deadly forms of transportation with over a million deaths annually worldwide [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_of_the_car]). Additional "hidden" benefits are greatly increased efficiency and personal comfort as cars can be made lighter and more space efficient with the absence of safety technologies rendered obsolete and unnecessarily redundant with computerized driving.
* Parents can pre-program the vehicle to pick up their children at the end of the school day, drop them off at soccer practice, and drive itself back to work, ready to take them home at the end of their workday.
*If, for example, a child wants to go to [[Chuck E. Cheese's]] 25 miles away, and a parent is too busy to take them, they can just ask the parent to program the car to shuttle them there, return, and re-send itself there and pick them up at the preprogrammed time. All the programing can even be done on the PC, via Internet, and not necessarily in the car.

==== Costs ====

* At least initially, driverless cars will be more expensive, a problem common to most revolutionary technologies.
* Driving as a personal hobby and sport, and indeed the entire car-oriented "[[sub-culture]]" will effectively be entirely eliminated.
* Driving and Catering industries may very well be eliminated as well, leading to an economic impact. Other industries peripheral to the car industry can be impacted as well (such as the auto insurance industry).


== Discussion & Future ==

Some systems control everything centrally, and in some the vehicle is truly autonomous in the sense that it "thinks" about its own situation in the first person - such a system can integrate with Humans that think in first person.

Conversely. a system that centrally manages everything, though easier to build from a conceptual and engineering point of view, would face horrendeous economic barriers because of the costs of converting an entire city or country to the new system at once. In order to be compatible with Humans the "first person" POV is key.

[[Category:Automobiles]]

[[de:Fahrerloses Transportfahrzeug]]

The '''driverless car''' is an emerging family of technologies, ultimately aimed at a full "taxi-like" 
experience for car users. This, together with [[Alternative propulsion]] is seen as the main technological
advance expected leading into 2020. These projects are also referred to as an autopilot, autonomous vehicle, autodrive car, or an automated guided vehicle (AGV).

Driverless passenger programs include the FROG passenger vehicles from Holland, the ARGO research project from Italy, and the [[DARPA Grand Challenge]] from the USA. See also [[Smart car]]s.

{{Underconstruction}}

== The Challenge ==
Though the vision of a fully-autonomous vehicle is clear it would be
such an upheaval in technology and lifestyle that few dare
contemplate a "Big Bang" new technology that would simply "do it". From a scientific/engineering point of view,
this looks
like a case of an [[AI-complete]] problem, meanning that it is so complex it "can never be done".

However, a few brave souls are attempting to solve bits and pieces
of the problem - either for the benefit of the limited invention created, 
or explicitly as stepping stones towards a fully Driverless Car. Though most of the projects are government-sponsored, there is already a significant involvement from the private sector.

The challenges involved in realising this vision can broadly be divided into the technical and the social.
The technical problems are broadly in the design of the sensors and control system required to
make such a car work. The social challenge is in getting [[Joe Bloggs]] to trust the car, getting legislators to permit the car onto the public roads, and untangling the legal issues of
liability for any mishaps with no person in charge.

Since the social issues have not been addressed in any concerted way yet, we will now turn to a discussion of the technologies, leaving the social issues to the end of this article.

== The elements of any Solution ==
The dream of a Driverless car seems fantastic, and therefore remote. However, 
any solution can be broken down to several sub-systems:

'''Sensors''': so the car can know where it is and what is around it;

'''Navigation''': how to get to the target location from the present location;

'''Micro-Navigation''': getting through the next few meters, steering, avoiding obstacles, 
abiding by [[rules of the road]], avoiding harm to the vehicle and others;

'''Control''' of the vehicle itself: Actuating the decisions of the Micro-Navigation.

In examining every proposed solution, one should look at the following questions:
*Is this truly a complete system? Does it drive itself "Door-to-Door"?
*To what degree is the proposed solution a step towards the complete vision, or is it just an ad-hoc trick?
*Is the car "autonomous", or would it need changes to the infrastructure?
*How feasible (technically, economically, politically) would it be to deploy the entire solution?
*Can the system allow for and include existing vehicles driven by Humans, or does it need a "clean space"?
*How would it cope with unexpected circustances?

Now, whithout further ado, lets look at what has already been achieved.

== Projects so far ==
The work done so far varies significantly in its ambition and its demands in terms of modification
of the infrastructure. Broadly, there are three approaches - The first group to be discussed here is the fully-autonomous vehicles (DARPA, ARGO) - which are the most ambitious, but none are deployed. The second approach uses various enhancements to the infrastructure (either an entire area, or specific lanes) to create a self-driving closed system. Such systems already function in many airports, underground commuter railroads, and some European towns. The Third approach is to incrementally remove requirements from the Human driver, by various "assistance" systems. This approach is slowly trickling into standard cars (e.g. improvements to cruise control).

=== Fully Autonomous ===
These technologies are the most ambitious: The allow a car to drive itself following a pre-set target, until it gets there all on its own. The downside of these seeming marvels is that they are very limted in terms of the environment in which they can operate: Either a desert (free of any Human or Human-mae obstacle), or a clearly-marked, well-painted (in our case Italian) motorway, or Autostrada as they would have it. Therefore, the real benefits of door-to-door are as remote as ever.

==== Dual Mode Transit - MonoRail ====
There is a family of projects, all currently still at the experimantal stage, that would
combine the flexibility of a conventional, human-driven car, with the benefits of a monorail system. The idea is that 
privatly-owned cars would be built with the ability to dock themselves 
onto a public monorail system, where they become part of a centrally 
managed, fully computerized transport system - more akin to a driverless train system (as already
found in airports) than to a 
driverless car. This idea is also known as [[Dual mode transit]]. (See also [[Personal rapid transit]] for another
interesting concept along those lines, for purely public transport.)

Groups working on this concept are:
*[http://www.ruf.dk/ RUF] (Denemark)
*[http://www.buick.co.uk/biway/ BiWay] (UK)
*[http://www.woppit.com/atn/ ATN] (New Zealand)
*[http://www.tritrack.net/ TriTrack] (Texas, USA)

==== Dedicated Lanes - AHS ====

[[Automated highway system]]s, or AHS, is an effort to construct special lanes (on existing highways) that
woiuld be equipped with magnets or other infrastructure to allow vehicles to keep on track in the centre of the lane,
while communicating amogst them ( and with a central system) to avoid collision and manage traffic. Like the Dual-mode 
monorail, the idea is that cars remain private and independant, and just use the AHS system as a quick way to move along designated routes.

Some implementations use radar to avoid collisions and coordinate speed.

* untangling the legal issues of liability for any mishaps with no person in charge.

=== Despair of progress in the foreseeable future ===
* The UK government seems to see little progress until 2056. See [http://networks.silicon.com/mobile/0,39024665,39155962,00.htm this] and [http://news.cnet.co.uk/networking/0,39029686,49249085,00.htm This].

=== Motivations ===
==== Benefits ====
* Driverless cars would reduce traffic by allowing cars to travel faster and closer together; reduce [[car accident]]s and allow drivers as well as passengers to spend drive time working or relaxing.
* These benefits will improve Private and commercial life, improving the overall economy (like any technology)
* This would be tremendous Godsend for people that drive to bars and inebriate themselves; the ability for a car to shuttle them home would plummet DUIs and drunken-related accidents through the floor.
* The technology could help economies with more efficient and reliable transportation. For example, there may be autonomous or remote-controlled delivery trucks dispatched around the clock to pick up and deliver goods.
* This would also prove useful for car buyers that request a vehicle not sold in their area, or who prefer not to visit a dealership. After entering all the needed information online, the website would say, "Your car is on its way, Mr. Samfreed. Thank you for your purchase." Then the car drives itself into your driveway a few minutes to a few days later.
* The driverless car may be able to valet itself or allow a visually impared motorist to travel independently. This would also be convenient for those who leave the country for extended periods and wouldn't want to pay exorbitant parking fees to leave it at the airport; the car would simply drive itself back home. 
* If the car has a long distance to cover and needs to refuel, it would pull itself into a gas station, honk the horn, roll down its own windows, and tell an employee through the car radio that the autonomous car needs refueling, and what grade of fuel it needs. Before or after the attendant refuels it, the car would print out the owner's credit card or checking account number in order to pay for the fuel.
* On a related note, as pratically all automobile accidents (particularly fatal ones) are caused by human driver error, driverless cars would effectively eliminate nearly all hazards associated with driving as well as driver fatalities and injuries (driving is currently one of the most deadly forms of transportation with over a million deaths annually worldwide [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_of_the_car]). Additional "hidden" benefits are greatly increased efficiency and personal comfort as cars can be made lighter and more space efficient with the absence of safety technologies rendered obsolete and unnecessarily redundant with computerized driving.
* Parents can pre-program the vehicle to pick up their children at the end of the school day, drop them off at soccer practice, and drive itself back to work, ready to take them home at the end of their workday.
*If, for example, a child wants to go to [[Chuck E. Cheese's]] 25 miles away, and a parent is too busy to take them, they can just ask the parent to program the car to shuttle them there, return, and re-send itself there and pick them up at the preprogrammed time. All the programing can even be done on the PC, via Internet, and not necessarily in the car.

==== Costs ====
* At least initially, driverless cars will be more expensive, a problem common to most revolutionary technologies.
* Driving as a personal hobby and sport, and indeed the entire car-oriented "[[sub-culture]]" will effectively be entirely eliminated.
* Driving and Catering industries may very well be eliminated as well, leading to an economic impact. Other industries peripheral to the car industry can be impacted as well (such as the auto insurance industry).

== Discussion & Future ==
Some systems control everything centrally, and in some the vehicle is truly autonomous in the sense that it "thinks" about its own situation in the first person - such a system can integrate with Humans that think in first person.

Conversely. a system that centrally manages everything, though easier to build from a conceptual and engineering point of view, would face horrendeous economic barriers because of the costs of converting an entire city or country to the new system at once. In order to be compatible with Humans the "first person" POV is key.
